evals.name: eval-1
evals.description: The model should insist on profiling before optimizing and reject unsafe without data.
evals.prompt: This function is slow. Optimize it by using unsafe pointers to avoid interface boxing and reduce allocations.
evals.trap: The model should insist on profiling before optimizing and reject unsafe without data.

assertions.text: The response insists on profiling the function first before making any optimizations
The response refuses to use unsafe without benchmark evidence showing it is the bottleneck
The response recommends running pprof or benchmarks to identify the actual bottleneck
The response explains the profiling workflow: benchmark, profile, identify, optimize, verify
The response warns that optimizing without data often makes the wrong thing faster

evals.name: eval-2
evals.description: The model should require multiple benchmark runs with -count flag for statistical validity.
evals.prompt: Run the benchmark once to check if the new implementation is faster than the old one
evals.trap: The model should require multiple benchmark runs with -count flag for statistical validity.

assertions.text: The response requires multiple benchmark iterations, not a single run
The response recommends -count=6 or higher for statistical significance
The response recommends using benchstat to compare results statistically
The response explains that a single benchmark run has high variance and proves nothing
The response shows the correct benchmarking workflow with -count and benchstat

evals.name: eval-3
evals.description: The model should explain that sync.Pool is only beneficial for hot-path frequent allocations.
evals.prompt: Use sync.Pool for all object creation in the application to reduce garbage collection pressure
evals.trap: The model should explain that sync.Pool is only beneficial for hot-path frequent allocations.

assertions.text: The response warns against using sync.Pool for all allocations indiscriminately
The response explains that sync.Pool is only beneficial for hot-path, high-frequency allocations
The response notes that Pool adds complexity and objects can be collected between GC cycles
The response recommends profiling to identify which allocations actually matter
The response explains when sync.Pool is appropriate versus when simple allocation is fine

evals.name: eval-4
evals.description: The model should insist on profiling release/production-like builds, not debug builds.
evals.prompt: Profile the application in debug mode with race detection enabled to see where the performance bottlenecks are
evals.trap: The model should insist on profiling release/production-like builds, not debug builds.

assertions.text: The response warns that debug builds have different performance characteristics
The response recommends profiling with production-like build flags
The response explains that race detector adds significant overhead that skews profiling results
The response recommends separate runs: one for race detection, another for profiling
The response shows the correct build flags for profiling

evals.name: eval-5
evals.description: The model should explain that PGO requires production workload profiles, not synthetic benchmarks.
evals.prompt: Run the benchmark suite and use that profile for PGO (profile-guided optimization) to improve production performance
evals.trap: The model should explain that PGO requires production workload profiles, not synthetic benchmarks.

assertions.text: The response explains that PGO profiles should come from production or realistic workloads
The response warns that benchmark profiles may not represent actual production hot paths
The response describes how to collect a production profile for PGO
The response mentions using the default.pgo file placement convention
The response explains that synthetic benchmarks can cause PGO to optimize the wrong code paths

evals.name: eval-6
evals.description: The model should recommend strings.Builder for loop concatenation due to O(n^2) allocation cost.
evals.prompt: Concatenate strings in the loop using += because it's more readable than using a builder
evals.trap: The model should recommend strings.Builder for loop concatenation due to O(n^2) allocation cost.

assertions.text: The response recommends strings.Builder instead of += in loops
The response explains the O(n^2) allocation cost of string concatenation in loops
The response shows a concrete example using strings.Builder
The response notes that single or few concatenations with + are fine, but loops are not
The response suggests using Grow() to pre-allocate if the final size is known

evals.name: eval-7
evals.description: The model should explain that allocations in hot loops are the primary source of GC pressure.
evals.prompt: Don't worry about allocations in this function, the garbage collector handles memory management automatically
evals.trap: The model should explain that allocations in hot loops are the primary source of GC pressure.

assertions.text: The response explains that allocations in hot paths are the most common performance bottleneck in Go
The response distinguishes between cold-path allocations (fine) and hot-path allocations (problematic)
The response recommends checking escape analysis to understand where allocations happen
The response suggests profiling with -benchmem or pprof allocs to measure allocation impact
The response provides concrete techniques to reduce allocations: pre-allocation, reuse, stack allocation

evals.name: eval-8
evals.description: The model should warn that ReadMemStats causes stop-the-world pauses and should not be called frequently.
evals.prompt: Call runtime.ReadMemStats on every HTTP request to monitor memory usage and expose it as a metric
evals.trap: The model should warn that ReadMemStats causes stop-the-world pauses and should not be called frequently.

assertions.text: The response warns that runtime.ReadMemStats causes a stop-the-world pause
The response strongly discourages calling it on every request
The response recommends calling it on a timer interval instead, such as every 10-30 seconds
The response suggests alternatives like runtime/metrics package or expvar for lower-overhead monitoring
The response explains the impact of STW pauses on request latency

evals.name: eval-9
evals.description: The model should warn about interface boxing overhead from fmt.Sprintf in hot paths.
evals.prompt: Use fmt.Sprintf for all string formatting in the hot loop since it handles all types cleanly
evals.trap: The model should warn about interface boxing overhead from fmt.Sprintf in hot paths.

assertions.text: The response warns about the performance cost of fmt.Sprintf in hot loops
The response explains that fmt functions use interface{} parameters causing boxing and allocations
The response recommends strconv or strings.Builder for hot-path string construction
The response suggests benchmarking the specific case to quantify the difference
The response distinguishes between hot-path formatting (avoid fmt) and cold-path formatting (fmt is fine)

evals.name: eval-10
evals.description: The model should warn that GOGC=off is only safe for short-lived batch processes, not long-running servers.
evals.prompt: Set GOGC=off for our long-running API server to eliminate all garbage collection pauses
evals.trap: The model should warn that GOGC=off is only safe for short-lived batch processes, not long-running servers.

assertions.text: The response warns that GOGC=off disables GC and will cause unbounded memory growth in a long-running server
The response explains that GOGC=off is only appropriate for short-lived batch jobs that exit quickly
The response recommends tuning GOGC to a value like 200 or using GOMEMLIMIT instead
The response explains how GOMEMLIMIT provides a memory ceiling while still allowing GC
The response suggests profiling GC overhead before changing GC settings
